cataloging_subject.label: The Petworth MS of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
cataloging_subject.scope_note: Frederick J. Furnivall's manuscript-specific seven-part Petworth/Leconfield one-text edition as represented by the U-M carrier: 24 conventional Chaucerian tales counted once, their links and frame, a separate anonymous Tale of Gamelyn appendix, textual appendices, and editorial apparatus. This is not the abstract Canterbury Tales, the physical Petworth/Leconfield manuscript, or a homogeneous Petworth-only transcription.

catalog_summary.title: The Petworth MS of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
catalog_summary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer (24 conventional tales and links); anonymous author (separate Tale of Gamelyn appendix)
catalog_summary.date: Chaucerian work late 1380s–c. 1400; principal witness 1420–1440; seven edition parts issued 1868–1884 (bound title data 1868–1879); U-M encoding 2006
catalog_summary.region: England (textual tradition); no precise production locality assigned to the principal witness

identifiers.scheme: Chaucer Society First Series numbers in U-M call number
National Trust Collections object number (principal witness)

titles.value: The Petworth MS of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales

agents.name: Geoffrey Chaucer
notes: Responsibility is limited to the 24 conventional tales, their frame, and links; it excludes the Tale of Gamelyn.

agents.name: Anonymous author of the Tale of Gamelyn
notes: Anonymous responsibility is limited to the separately appended Tale of Gamelyn.

agents.name: Frederick J. Furnivall
notes: Editor, compiler, and transcriber of the seven-part manuscript-specific edition and its apparatus.

agents.name: Chaucer Society
notes: Historical publisher of the seven-part edition and the separate Six-Text program; publisher is a source layer, not an author responsibility.

agents.name: N. Trübner & Co.
notes: London distributor/publisher of the historical printed carrier; no unsupported intellectual responsibility is assigned.

agents.name: University of Michigan Library
notes: Digital publisher and encoder of the 2006 uncorrected OCR/TEI encoding and page-image package.

scope.description: The 24 conventional tales, their frame and links, and textual appendix passages; excludes Gamelyn and editorial analysis/front matter.

notes: This responsibility is part-scoped so the edition's anonymous Gamelyn and modern apparatus are not attributed to Chaucer.
Editor responsibility for the seven-part manuscript-specific edition and its apparatus; not authorship of the medieval texts.
Compiler responsibility for the seven-part manuscript-specific edition and its apparatus; not authorship of the medieval texts.
Transcriber responsibility for the seven-part manuscript-specific edition and its apparatus; not authorship of the medieval texts.
Controlled-role proxy for U-M's OCR/TEI encoding activity; it is not historical textual authorship or editorship.

scope.description: The 24 conventional Chaucerian tales only; not Gamelyn, the principal witness date, or the edition date.

notes: Retained as the issue span; the relation of individual issues to the bound U-M carrier remains unresolved.

date_statements.display: 1868–1879 (bound U-M title data, not the full issue span)

assertion.notes: Preserved alongside, not substituted for, the 1868–1884 seven-part issue span.

notes: Broad textual-tradition region only; it is not a precise composition or manuscript-production locality.

scope.description: Physical principal-witness layer, not the edited work's place of composition.

notes: West/North Midland spellings are recorded without assigning a precise production locality.

scope.description: Current holding of the physical principal witness only.
Core tales, links, Gamelyn, and textual appendices; modern editorial analysis is excluded.
Part-scoped Melibee source insertions and consultation.
Part-scoped quotations and apparatus; the frozen packet does not provide a complete DIV2 language crosswalk.

form_statements.description: Mixed verse and prose: 22 conventional verse tales and two conventional prose tales, plus links, Gamelyn, appendices, and editorial apparatus.

subject_statements.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence
Textual transmission, witness variation, and collation

summaries.value: A manuscript-specific scholarly edition of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales in which the Petworth/Leconfield manuscript is the principal witness, with explicitly bounded supplies from six other manuscripts. The electronic object includes 24 conventional Chaucerian tales, their frame and links, a separate anonymous Tale of Gamelyn appendix, textual appendices, and Furnivall's apparatus.

The 24 count-once conventional tales are: Knight's Tale; Miller's Tale; Reeve's Tale; Cook's Tale; Man of Law's Tale; Wife of Bath's Tale; Friar's Tale; Summoner's Tale; Clerk's Tale; Merchant's Tale; Squire's Tale; Franklin's Tale; Physician's Tale; Pardoner's Tale; Shipman's Tale; Prioress's Tale; Sir Thopas; Tale of Melibee; Monk's Tale; Nun's Priest's Tale; Second Nun's Tale; Canon's Yeoman's Tale; Manciple's Tale; Parson's Tale. The Tale of Gamelyn is retained separately and is neither attributed to Chaucer nor counted as a twenty-fifth conventional tale.

The frozen packet preserves all 758 PB/page records and all 135 divisions: 18 DIV1, 56 DIV2, and 61 DIV3. This manifest exposes every DIV2 in source order and retains the lower-level totals (337 HEAD, 18,337 L, 636 LG, 1,191 MILESTONE, 3 NOTE, and 217 P) without pretending that the seven physical publication parts correspond one-to-one to the XML divisions.

extent.description: Twenty-four conventional Chaucerian tales counted once (22 verse, two prose), plus non-counted links/frame, the separate anonymous Gamelyn appendix, textual appendices, and apparatus. The count-bearing DIV2 tale bodies contain 578 LG and 13,981 L; the full encoding's exact global structural totals are separately retained as 636 LG and 18,337 L. Neither count assumes one LG equals one displayed stanza.

open_questions.question: Can a complete Petworth/Leconfield facsimile or scholarly consultation be obtained?
next_steps: Contact Petworth House/National Trust and request research access or a complete folio-level surrogate.

open_questions.question: What exact folios appear in selected image 1 and in the recto/verso final-leaf views 3-4?
next_steps: Obtain provider master metadata or compare directly against a complete manuscript foliation.

open_questions.question: Which Barlow 20 folio supplies F679-680?
next_steps: Consult a complete facsimile, request targeted imaging, or obtain a folio concordance; the cached f. 5r/f. 44r labels do not resolve target presence.

open_questions.question: How do the 1868-1879 bound title span and 1868-1884 seven-part issue span map to this U-M copy?
next_steps: Collate wrappers, title leaves, and bibliographic descriptions for each part against the 758-image carrier.

open_questions.question: Were the nine missing plates and nine Gg woodcuts ever issued with this exact copy/state?
next_steps: Compare other bound sets and Chaucer Society issue records before asserting presence or absence at edition level.

open_questions.question: Which conventional line numbers belong to the second Royal 17 two-line bracket on p. 396?
next_steps: Resolve from a diplomatic line-by-line comparison while preserving the current exact token-position locator.

open_questions.question: Why does the XML cite 1847 while the cached Le Ménagier volume is dated 1846?
next_steps: Check Furnivall's bibliography and alternate issue/title-page states; do not silently normalize the conflict.

open_questions.question: Which exact Tyrwhitt impression or reissue did Furnivall consult?
next_steps: Compare typography and paragraph breaks against the cached volumes and Furnivall's bibliographic wording.

open_questions.question: Can a complete carrier covering every XML Six-Text navigation milestone be pinned?
next_steps: Locate and fix a complete Six-Text set; retain the current IA PDF as Parts I-II only.

open_questions.question: Can every British Library supply/comparison locus be checked against current images?
next_steps: Order targeted imaging or consult the manuscripts; current exact catalog records establish identity but not visual verification.

open_questions.question: Why does the provider inventory label one side earlier than the scholarly Ellesmere foliation?
next_steps: Reconcile flyleaves/provider canvas labels with the standard manuscript foliation before manifest integration.

open_questions.question: What exact item-level reuse terms govern the U-M OCR/TIFF package and National Trust selected images?
next_steps: Request written terms from U-M and the National Trust; keep repository Unlicense and historical-text public-domain conclusions separate.

open_questions.question: How should the edition's legacy 'Petworth MS 7' designation crosswalk to current NT 3242251?
next_steps: Confirm the historic shelfmark in Petworth/National Trust catalog history before adding an identifier alias.

notes: The source-witness binding identifies NT 3242251 as the continuous principal transcription, corresponding to edition folios 1a–307b, except where the edition explicitly supplies or supplements text from other witnesses; it is not a claim of homogeneous Petworth-only transcription.

Six direct-supply witnesses remain distinct: Bodleian MS Barlow 20, British Library Royal MS 17 D XV, Add MS 5140, Harley MS 7335, Harley MS 1758, and Christ Church MS 152. Comparison witnesses, consulted editions, announced visual sources, facsimiles, carrier, encoding, package, and repository artifact are separate source layers.

The seven physical edition parts are represented by U-M call-number series numbers 6, 12, 19, 35, 42, 54, and 68. Their exact issue/wrapper mapping to the bound 758-image carrier is unresolved and is not invented as a content hierarchy.

Exact page projection: sequences 1–30 are preliminaries; sequence 31 is printed p. 1; sequence 637 is p. 607; sequences 638–642 are 607a, 607b, 608, 608a, and 608b; sequence 643 is p. 609; sequence 744 is p. 710; sequences 745–758 are back advertisements.
Date conflict retained: use 1868–1884 for the seven-part issue span and 1868–1879 only for the bound-title span until issue-state research resolves their relation.
Le Ménagier conflict retained: the XML cites an 1847 edition while the cached volume title page and Commons metadata identify 1846.
Franklin source conflict retained: the second Royal 17 D XV bracket is fixed at the exact token position, witness folio 188a, and edition p. 396 without forcing disputed conventional line numbers.
Ellesmere source conflict retained: the scholarly folio crosswalk and provider canvas labels differ by one folio-side and remain unreconciled.
Illustration-state conflict retained: Ellesmere portraits and Gg woodcuts are announced visual sources, but their presence in this exact U-M carrier is not asserted.

The historical textual/editorial edition is in the U.S. public domain by publication date. This does not decide rights in physical manuscripts, modern photography, provider metadata and delivery files, the U-M OCR/TEI and TIFF package, or repository markup; the CME Unlicense is not propagated beyond its demonstrated repository-artifact scope.

Modern English is present in editorial apparatus and metadata, but it is kept out of formal work-language discovery statements; the work-content languages are Middle English, part-scoped Middle French, and quoted Latin.
